reasoning: Estimating the probability of exactly 'no change' in Fed interest rates at a specific meeting over a year away involves significant uncertainty. Historically, the base rate for no change at any given FOMC meeting has been around 50-60%, but March meetings (which include a Summary of Economic Projections) are often prime candidates for policy adjustments if the Fed is actively recalibrating rates. Given the timeline out to March 2026, the market currently anticipates the Fed will eventually settle into a new neutral rate. The probability of 'no change' is lower than a random meeting because of the SEP focus. However, during periods of normal economic stability, rates are often held constant for consecutive meetings. Due to the high uncertainty of when the current adjustment cycle will end and the typical volatility in forward-looking rate path anticipations, a probability of 35% reflects the base rate of inaction mixed with the relatively frequent adjustments historically seen at quarter-end SEP meetings.
